attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- less-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
A few common causes:

**Tethering issues:**
- Capture One 16.x dropped legacy tethering support for some Canon models — check their [supported cameras list](https://www.captureone.com/en/support/supported-cameras) to confirm the 5D Mark IV is still listed
- Canon requires their **EOS Utility** driver installed even when using Capture One

**Quick checklist:**
1. EOS Utility installed and up to date?
2. Camera set to **PTP** mode (not MTP) in connection settings
3. Try a different USB cable — preferably the official Canon one
4. Camera firmware up to date?
5. Quit EOS Utility before launching Capture One (they conflict)

**macOS-specific:** If you're on macOS 26, there may be new USB permission prompts blocking the connection — check System Settings > Privacy & Security.

What OS are you on, and what version of Capture One?

I can't browse the web or check Capture One's site directly — I'd need you to run a web search.

You can try: `! open "https://www.captureone.com/en/support/supported-cameras"`

Or just Google **"Capture One 16 Canon 5D Mark IV tethering"** — the support page and community forums usually have the clearest answer.

content.text:
Found it. **Two things are conflicting:**

1. **EOS Webcam Utility system extension** is running (`com.canon.cusa.eoswebcam`) — this is grabbing the camera and blocking Capture One
2. **EOS Utility** is likely also open holding the connection

**Fix:** Quit EOS Utility if it's open, then disable the webcam extension temporarily:

```bash
# Kill the webcam extension
sudo systemextensionsctl disable com.canon.cusa.eoswebcam.cameraExtension
```

Or the easier route: go to **System Settings > General > Login Items & Extensions > Camera Extensions** and disable the Canon EOS Webcam one, then reconnect in Capture One.

content.text:
Found the issue. Capture One is looking for the camera but **macOS Image Capture daemon (`mscamerad`) is holding the USB connection**, blocking Capture One's PTP access.

**Fix — open Image Capture and release the camera:**

1. Open **Image Capture** (in Applications)
2. Select your Canon 5D Mark IV in the sidebar
3. Bottom-left corner — click the dropdown next to "Connecting this camera opens:" and set it to **No application**
4. Then try tethering in Capture One again

Or do it via terminal:

input.description: Disable Image Capture auto-launch on camera connect

content.text: Done. Now **unplug and replug** your camera — that should stop macOS from grabbing it, letting Capture One connect via PTP. Try tethering again.
Let me dig deeper into what's actually holding the camera.

input.command: lsof | grep -i "canon\|camera\|EOS\|ptp" 2>/dev/null | grep -v grep && echo "---" && system_profiler SPUSBDataType 2>/dev/null | grep -B2 -A15 "Canon"
input.description: Check what processes have the camera open and USB details

content.text:
Found the culprits. **Three processes are holding the camera simultaneously:**

- `ptpcamerad` (macOS PTP daemon — owns the USB connection)
- `EOSWebcamService` (EOS Webcam Utility — using Canon EDSDK on the camera)
- Two `mscamerad` instances

Kill them all, then try Capture One:

input.description: Kill ptpcamerad, EOSWebcamService, and mscamerad processes holding the camera

content.text:
Now **unplug and replug** the camera, then immediately go to Capture One and try to connect before those daemons restart and grab it again.

If it still doesn't work, the longer-term fix is to fully uninstall EOS Webcam Utility — it's the main offender using Canon's EDSDK to monopolize the camera.
Did it still not connect, or are you saying no to uninstalling EOS Webcam Utility?
Let me check if those daemons respawned and grabbed the camera again.

content.text:
There's the real issue — **macOS 26 (26.2)** is extremely new and Capture One 16.7.8 almost certainly hasn't been updated for it yet. Tethering relies on low-level camera APIs that Apple frequently changes between major macOS versions.

This is a Capture One compatibility problem, not a settings problem. Options:

1. **Check Capture One's release notes** — they usually push a compatibility update within weeks of a new macOS release. You may need to wait for 16.8 or later.

2. **Check their forums** at community.captureone.com — others on macOS 26 will be reporting the same issue.

3. **Workaround in the meantime**: shoot tethered using EOS Utility and use a watched folder in Capture One (Camera > Tether without direct connection).
I can't browse the web — I don't have internet access. You'd need to check those yourself, or type `! open "https://community.captureone.com"` to open the forum directly.

message.content: CAN I DOWNGRADE TO USE A DIFFEREWNT OS?

content.text:
Yes, but it's not simple. A few options:

**Easiest — check if you have a Time Machine backup** from before you upgraded to macOS 26. If so, you can restore from it.

**Without a backup:**
- You'd need to erase and reinstall an older macOS (like Sequoia 15) via Recovery Mode
- You'd lose all data unless you back up first

**Before doing anything drastic** — do you actually need macOS 26 for anything else? The easier move is just using EOS Utility's watched folder workaround until Capture One patches it. Downgrading a Mac is painful.

What macOS were you on before upgrading?

Yeah not worth it. Just shoot tethered through EOS Utility for now — it sees the camera fine — and point Capture One at the same folder to auto-import. Not ideal but it works until Capture One drops a patch for macOS 26.

Yeah EOS Utility is your best bet for now. Set it up like this:

1. In EOS Utility → **Remote Shooting** → set the save folder
2. In Capture One → **File** → **Hotfolder** → point it at that same folder

Shots will auto-import into Capture One as you fire. Not as seamless as native tethering but gets the job done.
